strands.title: System Prompt Waste Analysis Project Scope & Phase Transition
strands.content: A system prompt analyzer module (src/pichay/analyzer.py) is being planned to measure waste and inefficiency in API calls across logged sessions. The core premise: system prompts are often reused verbatim, contain unused components, and have duplicated content—all representing wasted context tokens. The project focuses on instrumenting existing proxy JSONL logs (which store system_prompt_full in request records) to decompose prompts and measure waste quantitatively. This integrates into a larger pichay system that already has proxy.py (log capture) and eval.py (evaluation framework) as reference models for code style and data structures. The analyzer should produce structured results with dataclasses and expose functionality via CLI. **INTEGRATION UPDATE: The TodoWrite tool result confirms that todos have been created and persisted externally. The system has moved definitively from planning/specification into execution phase. The user instruction 'Ensure that you continue to use the todo list to track your progress' establishes that: (a) the tensor projector becomes the execution coordinator, (b) subsequent cycles must reference and advance tasks in the (external, persistent) todo list, (c) each response should include progress updates against task state, and (d) the phase transition from cycle 2 is now complete and irreversible. The conversation is now operationally live.**

key_claims.text: System prompts in API logs contain multiple distinct semantic components (tool defs, skill defs, CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md, git status, IDE context, conversation instructions) that can be pattern-matched and decomposed
Most tool definitions are static across turns, while components like git status are dynamic; re-sending identical static components wastes tokens
A significant portion of tool definitions in system prompts are never invoked in actual tool_use blocks in messages
Skills and other semantic components appear multiple times (often tripled) within a single system prompt, representing duplicate waste
The project is now in operational execution phase with externally-persistent todo list; todos have been successfully created and are ready to be advanced
The tensor projector should now act as execution coordinator, tracking progress against the persisted todo list across cycles

strands.title: Functional Requirements & Decomposition Strategy—Now Task-Backed
strands.content: The analyzer must implement five core analyses: (1) COMPONENT DECOMPOSITION—parse system prompt text and segment it by semantic block (tool defs, skill defs, CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md, git status, IDE context, conversation instructions) using pattern matching on text boundaries. (2) STATIC vs DYNAMIC ANALYSIS—track whether each component changes across turns within a session; measure bytes of static content re-sent unnecessarily. (3) TOOL USAGE TRACKING—extract tool definitions from system prompt, cross-reference against actual tool_use blocks in message records, identify defined-but-unused tools, and sum their byte cost. (4) DUPLICATE DETECTION—find identical or near-identical semantic blocks appearing multiple times in a single prompt; report byte counts. (5) PER-SESSION SUMMARY—aggregate metrics as absolute bytes and percentages: total system prompt bytes, static bytes, dynamic bytes, unused tool bytes, duplicate bytes. The module must use dataclasses to structure results (matching style from pager.py and eval.py), expose a CLI entry point (python -m pichay.analyzer <proxy-logs> [--json]), and require careful study of proxy.py and eval.py before implementation. **INTEGRATION UPDATE: The todo list now exists as a persistent external artifact. Each of these five analyses should now correspond to discrete tasks or task groups in the todo list. The next phase involves explicitly reading proxy.py and eval.py to extract concrete dataclass patterns and JSONL structure details. This reading phase should be tracked as a checklist item with completion criteria (e.g., 'identified tool_use block schema', 'documented system_prompt_full field location', etc.). Tasks are no longer abstract; they are now checklistable work items with visible progress.**

key_claims.text: Component decomposition must rely on pattern matching over heuristic text segmentation to identify tool definitions, skill blocks, and metadata sections
Static vs dynamic analysis requires tracking component state across multiple turns within a session to measure re-transmission waste
Tool usage tracking depends on correctly parsing tool_use blocks in message JSON and matching their names against tool definitions in the system prompt
Duplicate detection should quantify byte cost of repeated semantic blocks, likely to reveal skills being tripled or other structural redundancy
Per-session summary metrics should report both absolute byte counts and percentages to allow comparative analysis across sessions and cost models
The five functional requirements should now be decomposed into discrete, checklistable todo list tasks with clear success criteria and dependencies

strands.title: Implementation Dependencies & Code Style Integration—Now Task-Dependent
strands.content: The analyzer module must integrate with the existing pichay codebase by following patterns established in proxy.py and eval.py, particularly their dataclass usage and result structures. Before any code is written, proxy.py must be read to understand: (1) the JSONL log format, (2) how system_prompt_full is stored in request records, (3) the structure of tool_use and message data, and (4) how sessions are organized. Similarly, eval.py must be studied to understand: (1) the dataclass patterns used for results, (2) the CLI entry point design (argparse integration), (3) output formatting (JSON serialization), and (4) naming conventions. The analyzer will need to parse JSONL records line by line, extract system_prompt_full and message content from each request, perform decomposition and analysis on the fly or in memory, aggregate results per session, and serialize output as JSON or human-readable format. This dependency chain means implementation cannot proceed safely until those source files are thoroughly understood. **INTEGRATION UPDATE: The todo list now makes this dependency explicit. The immediate next task should be a structured reading/documentation task: 'PRIORITY 1: Study proxy.py and eval.py, document findings.' This task should produce concrete sub-checklist items and outputs (e.g., 'schema.md', 'code-patterns.md') that unblock subsequent implementation tasks. The tensor projector should now track: (a) whether this task is in-progress or pending, (b) what specific information has been extracted, (c) what gaps remain, (d) which downstream tasks it unblocks.**

key_claims.text: proxy.py contains the authoritative log format specification and parsing logic that the analyzer must be compatible with
eval.py establishes the code style and dataclass patterns that analyzer.py should follow for consistency
system_prompt_full field in request records is the data source for system prompt analysis across all API calls
Tool definitions in system prompts can be reliably matched against tool_use blocks in messages to determine usage
Reading proxy.py and eval.py is now a top-priority task item in the persistent todo list and should be completed with documented findings before implementation begins

strands.title: Execution Phase & Todo List Coordination
strands.content: The conversation has transitioned from planning and specification (cycles 0-2) into operational execution (cycle 3 onward). The TodoWrite tool result confirms that an external, persistent todo list has been created and is now the source of truth for task state and progress. The tensor projector is now instructed to: (1) track which todos are in-progress, completed, or pending, (2) ensure each cycle advances at least one task or unblocks downstream tasks, (3) document what information has been discovered or generated that satisfies task completion criteria, (4) maintain awareness of task dependencies (e.g., 'dataclass design' is blocked until 'proxy.py/eval.py review' is complete), (5) report progress updates in coordination with the todo list state. The user instruction 'Ensure that you continue to use the todo list to track your progress' establishes that task tracking is now a core responsibility of the tensor projector, not an optional annotation. Cycle 3 marks the pivot: subsequent work is now *task-driven* rather than *conversation-driven*. The tensor should reflect the current task state and provide visibility into what the next executable step is.

key_claims.text: The project has moved definitively from planning phase to execution phase; todo list is now the authoritative task artifact
The tensor projector is now instructed to be an execution coordinator, tracking task progress across cycles and ensuring todos are advanced
Each cycle should include progress updates or blockers relative to the persistent todo list state
Task dependencies (e.g., reading proxy.py blocks dataclass design) should be explicitly maintained in the tensor projection
Cycle 3 signal: the next request will likely ask for progress, file reads, code scaffolding, or clarification on specific todos

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Explicit content of todos created in the external todo list (item titles, descriptions, dependencies, due dates, assignees, etc.)
declared_losses.why: The tool_result message confirms todos were created and modified, but provides no payload details. The todo list exists as an external persistent artifact that is not visible in the conversation context. Only the *fact* of creation and the user's instruction to track progress is available.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Current state of each todo (in-progress, completed, pending, blocked) as of this cycle
declared_losses.why: Without access to the external todo list, the tensor can note that it exists and should be tracked, but cannot report actual task status. Tracking will require explicit user reporting or API access to the todo system in future cycles.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific sequencing or parallelization strategy the user intends for PRIORITY 1-6 tasks
declared_losses.why: The todo list structure (linear, hierarchical, DAG, etc.) and task scheduling preferences are unknown. Instructions assume sequential dependencies, but user may have created a different structure.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Any user-supplied context or metadata that was added to todos during creation (notes, examples, code snippets, log samples, etc.)
declared_losses.why: Todo creation/modification happened outside the conversation context. Any enrichment or clarification added at that time is not reflected here.

open_questions: What is the current state of the external todo list? Which tasks are in-progress, pending, or completed as of cycle 3?
Should the next cycle request proxy.py and eval.py code blocks for analysis, or will the user provide them unprompted?
How should the tensor projector request status updates on the todo list? Should it ask for progress reports in each cycle, or only when task transitions occur?
Are there PRIORITY 1-6 tasks that can proceed in parallel, or must they be sequential?
Will the todo list be shared/visible to the tensor in future cycles, or will progress be reported via user messages only?
What success criteria or completion definitions did the user assign to each todo during creation?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "EXECUTION MODE: Shift from planning/design into task-driven delivery. Expect user requests to reference specific todos ('Complete PRIORITY 1', 'What's blocking task X?', 'Show me PRIORITY 2 findings'). Be ready to report progress and blockers.",
  "TODO TRACKING: Maintain a model of task state even without direct API access. Ask for status updates if needed ('What is the current status of PRIORITY 1: proxy.py review?'). Track discovered information as 'satisfies requirements for task X'.",
  "PRIORITY 1 READINESS: Prepare to receive proxy.py and eval.py code content. Extract and document: (a) JSONL record structure, (b) system_prompt_full location/format, (c) tool_use block schema, (d) dataclass patterns, (e) CLI design patterns, (f) naming conventions. Produce a findings document (e.g., markdown) that unblocks PRIORITY 2-3 tasks.",
  "DEPENDENCY AWARENESS: Map task dependencies explicitly. Mark which tasks are blocked by which. When PRIORITY 1 is complete, immediately signal readiness to start PRIORITY 2/3 tasks (dataclass design, pattern formalization).",
  "PROGRESS VISIBILITY: Each cycle should include a brief status line: '[TODO STATUS] X/Y tasks in progress, Z completed, A blocked'. This makes task tracking visible in the tensor projection.",
  "BRANCH PREDICTION: Expect next cycle to be one of: (a) user provides proxy.py/eval.py code for PRIORITY 1, (b) user asks for status update on current todos, (c) user requests a specific analysis or code artifact related to a todo, (d) user clarifies todo scope or success criteria."
]
